independence.approach: After drafting, a separate checklist-driven pass reopened the foundation, revision-bound source record and snapshots, media provenance, generated attribution, and all learner-facing claims. It tested each high-level fact and license assertion against retained evidence and searched for common CRISPR overgeneralizations rather than accepting drafting notes.

checks.note: FOUNDATION.json names Yu Wang—not an AI-agent label—as the accountable publisher, curator, and confirmed rights holder, and accurately discloses the limited human role in this automatically generated demonstration.
All five Wikipedia sources are bound to raw API snapshots, extracted-text hashes, page and revision IDs, timestamps, permanent URLs, retrieval metadata, and word counts.

Every declared text source has a pre-ingestion receipt bound to a retained, hashed Wikimedia Terms of Use snapshot; the deterministic process-notice scan found no unresolved notice, and pre-ingestion assurance passed before fetching the corpus.

The bacteriophage photograph is recorded as CC BY-SA 3.0 and the two U.S. National Institutes of Health photographs as public domain; all three have exact local provenance rows and retained, hashed Commons description-page evidence. Six SVG diagrams are identified as original contributions by Yu Wang.

CC BY-SA 4.0 satisfies the adapted Wikipedia text obligation and is compatible with the original diagrams, public-domain photographs, and the CC BY-SA 3.0 photograph under Creative Commons' later-version compatibility. The manifest, foundation, LICENSE, chapter records, and attribution agree.

Generated ATTRIBUTION.md mirrors structured provenance. Relevant learner-facing deliverables credit Wikipedia contributors, linked article identities, adaptation, CC BY-SA 4.0, and the exact revision record; media credits name creator, source, and license.

A separate claim audit checked CRISPR-system diversity; adaptation, biogenesis, and interference; spacer/protospacer/PAM distinctions; Type II and SpCas9-specific language; targeting checkpoints; active Cas9, nickase, and dCas9; repair outcome distributions; delivery exposure; assay boundaries; and empirical versus normative governance claims. Overstatements were removed or explicitly bounded.

keyFacts.claim: CRISPR–Cas systems are diverse microbial defense systems, so CRISPR should not be treated as the name of one protein or as a synonym for Cas9.
keyFacts.note: The source distinguishes CRISPR loci, multiple system types, and associated proteins. Every carrier confines detailed Cas9 mechanics to Type II or engineered Cas9 contexts.

keyFacts.claim: A useful CRISPR defense model separates spacer acquisition, guide-RNA biogenesis, and interference.
keyFacts.note: The revision-bound CRISPR article describes adaptation, biogenesis, and interference separately; the package avoids implying that acquisition occurs during every targeting event.

keyFacts.claim: A spacer is stored in a CRISPR array, its corresponding protospacer is located in target nucleic acid, and a PAM is a neighboring system-dependent motif rather than simply part of the guide.
keyFacts.note: The package explicitly rejects a universal PAM rule and labels 5′-NGG-3′ only as a common SpCas9 example.

keyFacts.claim: Cas9 action depends on PAM recognition, guide–target pairing, conformational progression, and catalysis; a sequence match alone does not prove cleavage.
keyFacts.note: Learner-facing text treats targeting as checkpoints and makes mismatch effects context-dependent instead of inventing a universal mismatch cutoff.

keyFacts.claim: Nuclease-active Cas9, Cas9 nickase, and catalytically inactive dCas9 retain different combinations of guide-directed binding and strand-cleavage activity.
keyFacts.note: The package distinguishes double-strand cleavage, single-strand nicking in the simplified variant model, and programmable binding without cleavage.

keyFacts.claim: A programmed DNA lesion creates an opportunity for cellular repair and can yield a distribution of alleles rather than one commanded genotype.
keyFacts.note: The package does not label all end joining as uniformly erroneous and presents NHEJ, microhomology-mediated joining, and template-supported repair as context-dependent pathway families.

keyFacts.claim: Base and prime editing expand the design space and can avoid a programmed double-strand break in many designs, but still require outcome and consequence validation.
keyFacts.note: The wording avoids claiming that non-DSB approaches are universally precise, safe, or free of mixed products and delivery effects.

keyFacts.claim: On-target efficiency, on-target heterogeneity, off-target activity, functional effect, and toxicity are different quality dimensions and cannot substitute for one another.
keyFacts.note: The package uses an evidence ladder and states the limits of short target-site amplicon sequencing before broader functional claims.

keyFacts.claim: Somatic, heritable, agricultural, and ecological editing contexts raise different empirical, ethical, consent, reversibility, and governance questions.
keyFacts.note: The package does not provide clinical advice or let technical accuracy settle normative decisions; empirical and normative claims are explicitly separated.
